title: Energy Sources — Nuclear Fusion and Beyond
data_note: Energy density values from standard nuclear/particle physics references. NIF data from LLNL press releases. Deuterium abundance from oceanographic surveys.

statistics.context: Baseline reference for energy density comparison. Hydrogen-oxygen combustion represents the highest chemical energy density.
statistics.verification_note: Standard thermochemical value; widely reproduced in literature

statistics.context: Approximately 6 × 10⁶ times chemical combustion energy density. Basis of commercial nuclear fission power.
statistics.verification_note: Cross-referenced with IAEA Nuclear Data Services

statistics.context: Approximately 2.4 × 10⁷ times chemical. Highest verified energy density of any known fuel source. NIF demonstrated ignition.
Eliminates tritium supply bottleneck but requires ~10× higher plasma temperature (~10⁹ K vs ~10⁸ K).
Produces no neutrons (3 alpha particles + 8.7 MeV). Enables direct energy conversion. Requires ~10⁹ K.
statistics.verification_note: Standard nuclear physics value; cross-section data from ENDF

statistics.context: Theoretical maximum for mass-to-energy conversion. Antimatter production rate globally is ~nanograms/year.

statistics.metric: DT fusion reaction energy per event
statistics.context: 3.5 MeV to alpha particle, 14.1 MeV to neutron (80% of energy carried by neutron).
statistics.verification_note: Extensively measured in nuclear physics experiments

statistics.context: First scientific breakeven: 2.05 MJ laser input → 3.15 MJ fusion output. Wall-plug gain still far below 1 (~300 MJ electrical input).
statistics.verification_note: LLNL official announcement; independently confirmed by DOE

statistics.context: 2.08 MJ laser input → 8.6 MJ fusion output. Progressive improvement from Dec 2022 baseline.
~300 MJ electrical → ~2 MJ laser delivered to target. Wall-plug efficiency ~0.7%.

source.name: Oceanographic data (1 in 6420 hydrogen atoms is deuterium)

statistics.context: Deuterium is abundant: 1 in every 6,420 hydrogen atoms in ocean water.
statistics.verification_note: IAEA isotope hydrology data; standard oceanographic measurement

source.name: Derived from ocean volume and deuterium concentration

statistics.context: Enough to power DD fusion for billions of years at current global energy consumption rates.
statistics.verification_note: Calculated from ocean volume (~1.335 × 10⁹ km³) and deuterium concentration

statistics.metric: Global tritium production from CANDU reactors
statistics.context: Tritium supply bottleneck is a key limitation for DT fusion. Half-life of 12.3 years further constrains stockpiling.
statistics.verification_note: Estimated from published CANDU heavy water reactor data; exact figures vary by source

statistics.context: Radioactive decay: ³H → ³He + e⁻ + ν̄ₑ. Constrains tritium stockpiling for DT fusion.
Naive QFT calculation. 10¹²⁰ times larger than observed cosmological constant — the cosmological constant problem.
statistics.verification_note: Theoretical calculation; the discrepancy with observation is itself well-verified

statistics.context: One of the deepest unsolved problems in physics. Ratio of theoretical vacuum energy to observed dark energy.
statistics.verification_note: Well-characterized discrepancy; independently confirmed by multiple analyses
